growth_records.strain: WITS (wild-type isogenic tagged strains), i.v. infection of wild-type C57BL/6 mice
growth_records.medium: In vivo - mouse spleen and liver (systemic infection after i.v. inoculation, ~80-100 CFU, ~93 bacteria)
growth_records.cite: Grant et al. Modelling within-Host Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Invasive Bacterial Disease. PLoS Biology (2008). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.0060074. [PMC2288627]
growth_records.snippet: the slow decline in bacterial numbers during the first 6 h p.i., is caused by the combined occurrence of high division rates (doubling times around 1.7 h in both organs) and higher death rates (half-lives around 1.1 h).
growth_records.notes: In vivo host-tissue infection, not a defined culture medium - included per instructions to capture every reported/calculable growth-rate observation, but likely not directly usable to constrain a GEM growing on a defined medium. Doubling time (1.7 h) is explicitly reported (branching-process model fit to WITS depletion data, 0.5-6 h p.i., spleen and liver of wild-type mice). Growth rate calculated as mu=ln2/1.7=0.41/h. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature]

growth_records.strain: WITS (wild-type isogenic tagged strains), i.v. infection of wild-type C57BL/6 mice
growth_records.medium: In vivo - mouse spleen and liver (systemic infection after i.v. inoculation)
growth_records.cite: Grant et al. Modelling within-Host Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Invasive Bacterial Disease. PLoS Biology (2008). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.0060074. [PMC2288627]
growth_records.snippet: bacterial mortality in the organs must be very low during this period, so that observed division rates (doubling times around 8 h in both organs) must decrease to reduce the overall observed bacterial growth (3- to 4-fol
growth_records.notes: In vivo host-tissue infection (same caveats as the 1.7 h record). Doubling time (8 h) explicitly reported for the 6-24 h p.i. phase, spleen and liver of wild-type mice, low bacterial mortality. Growth rate calculated as mu=ln2/8=0.087/h. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature]

growth_records.notes: Same in-host model-fit growth rate as the other two pathogens from this paper (r~0.08/h, fitted by inspection to Fig 3B pathogen-load curves, shared value across species, not species-resolved). No defined medium/temperature/pH for the actual in-host growth.

growth_records.strain: not specified (lab S. Typhimurium used in E. coli/Salmonella synthetic community)
growth_records.cite: Muzafar et al. The strength of interspecies interaction in a microbial community determines its susceptibility to invasion. PLOS Biology (2024). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.3002889. [PMC11575764]
growth_records.snippet: The values of rE, rS, and rC are based on empirically determined exponential growth rates for E. coli and S. Typhimurium in LB, which are approximately 0.033 min-1 for both these species. For a generation time of approxi
growth_records.notes: Same source value as the paired E. coli record: paper states the empirically determined exponential growth rate (0.033 min-1) is approximately the same for both E. coli and S. Typhimurium in LB. Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.033 min-1 * 60 = 1.98 h-1; doubling time = ln2/1.98 = 0.35 h, consistent with stated ~20 min generation time. Value attributed to cited prior work (refs 39,40), not a new measurement in this paper. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature]

growth_records.agit: fast and continuous shaking (microplate reader; rpm not stated)
growth_records.cite: Diard et al. A rationally designed oral vaccine induces Immunoglobulin A in the murine gut that directs the evolution of attenuated Salmonella variants. Nature Microbiology (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41564-021-00911-1. [PMC7611113]
growth_records.snippet: We use the model to predict the composition of a population after growth in LB overnight, and therefore set the specific growth rate to μ = 2.05/h, which corresponds to a doubling time of roughly 20min.
growth_records.notes: Specific growth rate μ=2.05 h^-1 stated explicitly in-text as the parameter used to model overnight batch growth of S.Tm SL1344 in LB; the paper itself cites this value from the literature (ref. 1, Fierer & Guiney 2001) rather than measuring it fresh in this study, so it is recorded as growth_method=reported (value as stated in the paper) with medium (not high) confidence. Doubling_time_h = ln2/2.05 = 0.338 h (~20.3 min), consistent with the paper's own statement of 'roughly 20 min'. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature]

growth_records.notes: Doubling time (88.8 +/- 21.2 min = 1.48 h) directly reported from exponential regression of bacterial fluorescent-voxel counts over time after FluidFM injection into the cytosol of wild-type intestinal enteroid cells (IECs). Not a conventional lab medium/culture (intracellular host-cytosol growth niche); no batch/chemostat mode applies. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature, oxygen]

growth_records.strain: SL1344 (wild-type strain SB300, an SL1344 derivative; WITS-tagged isogenic strains)
growth_records.medium: in vivo - mouse cecal lymph node (host tissue); C3H/HeN mice orally infected with S. Typhimurium (5x10^7 cfu by gavage)
growth_records.cite: Kaiser et al. Cecum Lymph Node Dendritic Cells Harbor Slow-Growing Bacteria Phenotypically Tolerant to Antibiotic Treatment. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.1001793. [PMC3928039]
growth_records.snippet: 298 bacterial cells enter the cLN, and that the bacterial population grows at a rate of 2.82 per day (corresponding to a population doubling time of 5.9 h; no ciprofloxacin applied during this period)
growth_records.notes: In vivo growth rate of S. Typhimurium SL1344 in mouse cecal lymph node (cLN) during the first day post-infection, before ciprofloxacin treatment. Paper states r=2.82/day and doubling time=5.9 h directly (both reported). mu = 2.82/24 h = 0.1175 h^-1 (curator cross-check, consistent with ln2/5.9h=0.1175 h^-1). Not a defined lab medium - this is a host-tissue (in vivo) measurement from a stochastic birth-immigration model fit to WITS-tagged strain counts. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature, oxygen]

growth_records.strain: SL1344 (wild-type strain SB300, an SL1344 derivative; WITS-tagged isogenic strains)
growth_records.medium: in vivo - mouse cecal lymph node (host tissue), during high-dose ciprofloxacin therapy (days 1-3 post-infection)
growth_records.cite: Kaiser et al. Cecum Lymph Node Dendritic Cells Harbor Slow-Growing Bacteria Phenotypically Tolerant to Antibiotic Treatment. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.1001793. [PMC3928039]
growth_records.snippet: During the first period of the treatment (days 1–3)...the apparent in vivo doubling time was about 3.6 h
growth_records.notes: Apparent in vivo doubling time = 3.6 h for the aggregate (susceptible+tolerant) S. Typhimurium subpopulation in the cLN during days 1-3 of ciprofloxacin treatment, as explicitly stated. mu = ln2/3.6 h = 0.1925 h^-1 (calculated by curator). Represents net apparent growth (replication minus clearance by antibiotic), not pure replication rate, per paper's own caveat. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature, oxygen]

growth_records.strain: SL1344 (wild-type strain SB300, an SL1344 derivative; WITS-tagged isogenic strains)

growth_records.snippet: the doubling time increased to 8.8 h (days 3–5) and 44 h between days 5 and 10 of the therapy
growth_records.notes: Doubling time = 8.8 h for the ciprofloxacin-tolerant S. Typhimurium subpopulation in cLN, days 3-5 of treatment (clearance rate now isolated to tolerant cells only). mu = ln2/8.8 h = 0.0788 h^-1 (calculated by curator). Independently corroborated elsewhere in the same paper by a plasmid-dilution (pAM34) method giving doubling time = 8.9 h for the tolerant phenotype (mu=ln2/8.9=0.0779 h^-1), consistent with this estimate. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature, oxygen]

growth_records.cite: Arnoldini et al. Bistable Expression of Virulence Genes in Salmonella Leads to the Formation of an Antibiotic-Tolerant Subpopulation. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.1001928. [PMC4138020]
growth_records.snippet: pumping speed was changed for three of the six reactors to be 384 ml/h (0.96 volume changes per hour, corresponding to a doubling time of 0.72 h)... Growth rates in the chemostats were 0.96 h−1 and 0.26 h−1 for 'fast' and 'slow', respective
growth_records.notes: 'Fast' chemostat. Dilution rate D = flow/volume = 384 ml/h / 400 ml = 0.96 h^-1. At steady state mu = D = 0.96 h^-1. Doubling time reported = 0.72 h (check: ln2/0.96 = 0.722 h). Paper matched this D to the single-cell T1- rate (see separate record). Medium = buffered LB, complex, composition not given (ref Ihssen et al 2004).

growth_records.cite: Arnoldini et al. Bistable Expression of Virulence Genes in Salmonella Leads to the Formation of an Antibiotic-Tolerant Subpopulation. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.1001928. [PMC4138020]
growth_records.snippet: fresh medium was pumped into the reactors at 104 ml/h (0.26 volume changes per hour, corresponding to a doubling time of 2.66 h), and total volume in the reactors was kept constant at 400 ml... Growth rates in the chemostats were 0.96 h−1 a
growth_records.notes: 'Slow' chemostat. Dilution rate D = 104 ml/h / 400 ml = 0.26 h^-1. At steady state mu = D = 0.26 h^-1. Doubling time reported = 2.66 h (check: ln2/0.26 = 2.666 h). Medium = buffered LB, complex, composition not given (ref Ihssen et al 2004).

growth_records.strain: SL1344 derivative (ΔfliCΔfljB psicA-gfp reporter); T1- (ttss-1 non-expressing) subpopulation
growth_records.cite: Arnoldini et al. Bistable Expression of Virulence Genes in Salmonella Leads to the Formation of an Antibiotic-Tolerant Subpopulation. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.1001928. [PMC4138020]
growth_records.snippet: single cell growth rates of T1 − and T1 + cells were determined to be 0.96 and 0.26 doublings per hour, respectively... corresponding to the rate at which the T1 − subpopulation grows, 0.96 doublings per hour
growth_records.notes: T1- (fast) subpopulation, single-cell in spent LB. Reported 0.96 doublings per hour. Doubling time = 1/0.96 = 1.04 h. mu = ln2 x doublings/h = 0.693 x 0.96 = 0.665 h^-1. NOTE: paper set chemostat D=0.96 h^-1 to 'match', but 0.96 doublings/h (td=1.04 h) actually corresponds to mu=0.665 h^-1, not 0.96 h^-1 (a unit conflation in the paper); values recorded as stated.

growth_records.strain: SL1344 derivative (ΔfliCΔfljB psicA-gfp reporter); T1+ (ttss-1 expressing) subpopulation
growth_records.cite: Arnoldini et al. Bistable Expression of Virulence Genes in Salmonella Leads to the Formation of an Antibiotic-Tolerant Subpopulation. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.1001928. [PMC4138020]
growth_records.snippet: single cell growth rates of T1 − and T1 + cells were determined to be 0.96 and 0.26 doublings per hour, respectively... the rate at which the T1 + subpopulation grows, 0.26 doublings per hour
growth_records.notes: T1+ (slow, virulence-expressing) subpopulation, single-cell in spent LB. Reported 0.26 doublings per hour. Doubling time = 1/0.26 = 3.85 h. mu = ln2 x doublings/h = 0.693 x 0.26 = 0.180 h^-1. Paper matched chemostat 'slow' D=0.26 h^-1 to this (unit conflation as noted for the T1- record).

growth_records.cite: Fanous et al. Limited impact of Salmonella stress and persisters on antibiotic clearance. Nature (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41586-024-08506-6. [PMC11882453]
growth_records.snippet: We set the inflow of fresh medium to 0.116 ml h-1 per ml of culture... (approximately 0.16 divisions per hour, generation time approximately 6 h). chemostat medium (100 mM MES, 5 mM KCl, 15 mM NH4Cl, 0.5 mM K2SO4, 1 mM KH2PO4, 50 uM MgSO4,
growth_records.notes: Standard tissue-mimicking chemostat, set division rate 0.17 doublings/h. mu = ln2 x 0.17 = 0.6931 x 0.17 = 0.1178 h-1; doubling_time = 1/0.17 = 5.88 h. Reported inflow/dilution 0.116 ml/h/ml (=D=mu at steady state, ~0.16 doublings/h, gen time ~6 h). Main text states 10 mM bicarbonate/pH 5.6; Methods (used here) states 25 mM NaHCO3/pH 5.5. No per-substrate uptake/secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.strain: LT2 delta-mutS (mismatch-repair-deficient hypermutator); weak-bottleneck (WBN) evolved cell lines

growth_records.snippet: WBN cultures of S. enterica were grown for ~840 generations, and SBN cultures of S. enterica were grown for ~1000 generations, equivalent to 18 and 22 generations between bottlenecks, respectively.
growth_records.notes: No measured uptake/secretion rates. Growth rate DERIVED: 18 generations per 12 h -> td = 12/18 = 0.667 h; mu = 0.693/0.667 = 1.040 ~ 1.04 /h. Average over passage interval (under-estimates mu_max). LB medium composition as stated for LB; glucose 0.2% added for S. enterica. Hypermutator strain; showed no fitness gain over evolution.

growth_records.medium: RPMI 1640 + 10% FCS + 10 mM glucose (intracellular in Caco-2 cells)

up.units: µmol assimilated per 1e8 intracellular bacteria per 8.5 h

growth_records.cite: Götz A et al. Carbon Metabolism of Enterobacterial Human Pathogens Growing in Epithelial Colorectal Adenocarcinoma (Caco-2) Cells. PLoS ONE (2010). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0010586. [PMC2868055]
growth_records.snippet: The 14C-label incorporated into the 10^8 intracellular Stm during the 8.5 h infection period corresponds to 0.1 µmol glucose ... Stm incorporated 5–8 times less per bacterial cell.
growth_records.notes: Growth rate CALCULATED from intracellular cfu (Table 1): mu=ln(1.1e8/3.1e7)/(8.5-1)h=ln(3.548)/7.5=0.169 /h; td=ln2/0.169=4.105 h (246 min) — consistent with text 'intracellular doubling time at least three-fold longer than EIEC'. Uptake=14C-glucose assimilation, 0.1 µmol per 1e8 bacteria per 8.5 h (5–8x less per cell than EIEC); not convertible to mmol/gDW/h (no dry weight). 13C profiling: Stm in SCV uses glucose (not glucose-6P/fatty acids) as major C source; qualitative pathway splits only.

growth_records.medium: RPMI 1640 + 10% FCS + 10 mM glucose (intracellular in Caco-2 cells)

growth_records.snippet: The replication rates of the glucose-6P uptake mutants of all three pathogens were comparable to those of the corresponding wild-type strains.

Comparable to Stm WT (0.169 /h) — glucose-6-phosphate not used intracellularly. No 14C glucose-uptake value reported for this mutant.

growth_records.snippet: Aerobic batch cultivation was done in an incubator shaker at 37C and 100 rpm. Minimal M9 medium was supplemented with 6 different carbon sources... gDW = 0.7396*OD600 ... experimental specific substrate uptake rates were calculated from HPL
growth_records.notes: GEM validation paper (iRR1083). Experimental aerobic-batch growth rates (calculated via Monod eqn from OD600) and experimental specific glucose uptake rate (from HPLC) WERE measured and compared to FBA predictions, but the numeric values appear only in Figure 1C (bar chart image) and Additional files, NOT in the machine-readable text — so no μ or qS number can be transcribed here without inventing. M9 exact salt concentrations not given ('contains salts, phosphate, sulfate, ammonium minerals'). Carbon source concentration not stated. Mixed-acid fermentation products (formate, acetate, ethanol, succinate) are cited from literature [35] for anaerobic conditions, not quantified in this aerobic study. Model used a generic 5 mmol/gDW/h max uptake bound for Biolog simulations (not an experimental measurement).

up.method: Experimental specific uptake from HPLC profiles; numeric values only in Figure 1C, not in text

growth_records.cite: Raghunathan, Anu et al. Constraint-based analysis of metabolic capacity of Salmonella typhimurium during host-pathogen interaction. BMC Systems Biology (2009). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-3-38. [PMC2678070]
growth_records.snippet: Growth of S. typhimurium on 3 pentoses (D-arabinose, D-ribose and D-xylose) was also tested experimentally. Growth was only observed on D-xylose and D-ribose... Minimal M9 medium was supplemented with 6 different carbon sources (glucose, fr
growth_records.notes: Additional carbon sources tested in aerobic M9 batch (37C). Qualitative growth outcomes: growth observed on glucose, fructose, glycerol, D-ribose, D-xylose; NO growth on D-arabinose (consistent with model). Experimental growth rates and substrate uptake rates were measured for each and plotted vs FBA predictions in Figure 1C, but numeric values are figure-only (not in text or extractable here). This record documents the distinct experimental conditions; per no-invention rule, μ and qS left null.

growth_records.cite: Baumler et al. The evolution of metabolic networks of E. coli. BMC Systems Biology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-5-182. [PMC3229490]
growth_records.snippet: S. typhimurium LT2 | 0.86 +/- 0.05 (aerobic mu_exp, Table 6).
growth_records.notes: Experimental aerobic mu_exp = 0.86 +/- 0.05 h-1 (Table 6). td=ln2/0.86=0.806 h. No strain-specific measured glucose uptake reported for LT2. MOPS minimal + 11 mM glucose.

growth_records.cite: Baumler et al. The evolution of metabolic networks of E. coli. BMC Systems Biology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-5-182. [PMC3229490]
growth_records.snippet: S. typhimurium LT2 | 0.54 +/- 0.01 (anaerobic mu_exp, Table 6).
growth_records.notes: Experimental anaerobic mu_exp = 0.54 +/- 0.01 h-1 (Table 6). td=ln2/0.54=1.284 h. No LT2-specific measured glucose uptake reported. MOPS minimal + 11 mM glucose.

growth_records.medium: Murine gut lumen (in vivo), C57BL/6 mice with low-complexity microbiota (LCM)
growth_records.cite: Evangelia Vayena et al. Metabolic network reconstruction as a resource for analyzing Salmonella Typhimurium SL1344 growth in the mouse intestine. PLOS Computational Biology (2025). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012869. [PMC11925469]
growth_records.snippet: We performed an iMM analysis for the experimentally observed growth rate of SL1344 in LCM mice that is equal to 0.12 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Experimentally observed in vivo growth rate of S. Typhimurium SL1344 in the gut lumen of low-complexity-microbiota (LCM) C57BL/6 mice = 0.12 h-1; used to constrain the biomass reaction of the iNTS_SL1344 GEM. This value derives from the authors' earlier experimental infection studies (cited refs 7 and 22), i.e. likely cited rather than newly measured here. doubling_time computed td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.12=5.78 h. NOTE: the uptake bounds used in this paper (max 25 mmol/gDW-h per carbon source; equimolar total-carbon cap 60 mmol C/gDW-h = 10 mmol glucose/gDW-h; total-nitrogen cap 2 mmol N/gDW-h = 2 mmol ammonia/gDW-h) are MODELING CONSTRAINTS/assumptions, not measured fluxes (is_experimental=false), so they are excluded from uptake_rates. In vitro single-carbon/single-nitrogen-source growth rates (aerobic and anaerobic) were measured from growth curves at exponential phase but the numeric values reside only in supplementary S4 Table, not in the main text, so no per-substrate in vitro records are emitted. body temperature 37C assumed (mouse host).

growth_records.strain: SL1344 (dual RNA-seq data source); experimental by-product rates cited for intracellular S. Typhimurium during epithelial-cell infection
growth_records.medium: Intracellular / DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium) host-cell environment during infection

experimental, CITED from ref [30]; model FBA predicted 6.80 mmol/gDW/h at 16 h

growth_records.cite: Kadir Kocabaş et al. Dual transcriptome based reconstruction of Salmonella-human integrated metabolic network to screen potential drug targets. PLoS ONE (2022). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0268889. [PMC9129043]
growth_records.snippet: Experimental (nM/cell/h) [30] D-Lactate Secretion ... 10 ± 3; Acetate Secretion ... 4 ± 2; Formate Secretion ... 2 ± 1; Succinate 0; Ethanol 0.
growth_records.notes: This is an FBA/genome-scale-model drug-target paper; its own uptake rates (glucose 5, O2 1 mmol/gDW/h) are MODEL CONSTRAINTS from literature (Thiele et al.; ref 61), NOT experimental. The only experimental physiology values are the Table 2 secretion rates in the 'Experimental (nM/cell/h) [30]' column, which are CITED verbatim from ref [30] = Garcia-Gutierrez E et al., 'A Comparison of the ATP Generating Pathways Used by S. Typhimurium to Fuel Replication within Human and Murine Macrophage and Epithelial Cell Lines', PLoS One 2016;11:e0150687 (doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0150687). Units nM/cell/h (per-cell) as reported. Model FBA predictions listed in mfa_fluxes for comparison (units mmol/gDW/h).
